text:
WHOLE BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY WITH SPECT/CT AND GALLIUM-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with a known history of metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5) with established osseous and pelvic nodal disease, previously treated with ADT plus abiraterone/prednisone, now on enzalutamide (approximately 7 months). Referred for confirmatory restaging imaging in the setting of progressive PSA elevation (PSA now 31.7 ng/mL, rising from a nadir of 1.9 ng/mL, with estimated doubling time of approximately 2.8 months). The patient reports new right hip pain and worsening lumbar back pain. Prior bone scintigraphy (6 months ago) demonstrated stable osseous disease at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala. Testosterone confirmed castrate. BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant known. Evaluate for radiographic progression.

TECHNIQUE:
Whole body planar Tc-99m MDP bone scintigraphy was performed following intravenous administration of approximately 925 MBq (25 mCi) of technetium-99m methylene diphosphonate, with imaging obtained at 3-hour post-injection delay. Supplemental SPECT/CT of the pelvis and lumbar spine was obtained for anatomic localization. Additionally, on the same day, Ga-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT was performed following intravenous administration of 185 MBq (5 mCi) of gallium-68-labeled PSMA ligand. Whole body PET imaging was obtained 60 minutes post-injection with co-registered low-dose CT attenuation correction. No IV contrast was administered for the PET/CT component.

COMPARISON:
Prior Tc-99m whole body bone scintigraphy and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis approximately 6 months prior.
Prior Tc-99m bone scintigraphy approximately 18 months prior (on abiraterone).

--- TC-99M MDP WHOLE BODY BONE SCAN FINDINGS ---

SKELETAL SYSTEM:

Cranium and facial bones: No abnormal areas of tracer uptake. No calvarial lesions.

Cervical spine: There is a new, small focus of mildly increased tracer uptake at the C7 vertebral level. SPECT correlation demonstrates this to be within the C7 vertebral body, without clear involvement of the posterior elements on planar imaging. This was not present on the prior bone scan.

Thoracic spine: New area of moderately increased tracer uptake involving the T11 vertebral body, clearly new compared to the prior study. Activity is focal and centrally placed within the vertebral body. No prior activity at this level.

Lumbar spine: Previously documented increased tracer uptake at L3 and L4 vertebral bodies is now clearly more prominent when compared to the study obtained six months ago, with both lesions demonstrating appreciably increased radiotracer accumulation. The L3 lesion in particular appears larger in extent and more intense. L4 shows similar worsening. These findings are consistent with active progression rather than the sclerotic/healing pattern observed on prior studies.

Sacrum and pelvis:
- Right sacral ala: Previously known area of increased uptake is again present, and appears mildly more intense compared to the prior examination.
- Right iliac crest: Previously documented lesion is again seen, with slightly increased activity compared to prior.
- Left acetabulum: New area of increased tracer uptake involving the left acetabular region, not identified on prior bone scan. SPECT/CT localizes this to the supra-acetabular portion of the left iliac wing/acetabular roof.

Femur:
- Right proximal femur: There is a new, moderately intense focus of tracer uptake in the right femoral neck extending into the intertrochanteric region. This is new and conspicuous compared to the prior study. Given the location in a weight-bearing segment, this is of particular clinical significance.
- Left femur: No abnormal uptake.

Remaining lower extremities, bilateral tibiae, fibulae, and feet: No suspicious areas of abnormal tracer accumulation.

Shoulder girdle, bilateral humeri, forearms, and hands: No abnormal foci of uptake. No upper extremity lesions.

Ribs and sternum: No convincing pathologic rib lesions. Mild symmetric costal cartilage calcification uptake is noted, consistent with age-related changes. No sternal abnormality.

Soft tissues: No abnormal extraskeletal tracer accumulation. Renal uptake symmetric and within normal limits. Bladder is well visualized.

SUPERSCAN ASSESSMENT: There is no evidence of a superscan pattern. The axial skeleton to appendicular skeleton ratio appears preserved.

--- GA-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT FINDINGS ---

HEAD AND NECK: No PSMA-avid foci within the brain parenchyma or calvarial structures. Normal physiologic PSMA expression noted in the bilateral salivary glands (parotid and submandibular), bilateral lacrimal glands, and bilateral kidneys/ureters. No cervical nodal PSMA-avid disease.

CHEST:
Lungs: No PSMA-avid pulmonary nodules or parenchymal lesions. Lung fields appear clear on the low-dose CT component. No pleural effusions or suspicious hilar adenopathy.
Mediastinum: No mediastinal adenopathy. No PSMA-avid mediastinal lesions.
Bone (thoracic): The T11 lesion identified on bone scan demonstrates corresponding moderate PSMA uptake (SUVmax approximately 9.2). The C7 focus, while faintly visible on bone scan, shows borderline PSMA activity that may represent early involvement.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: No PSMA-avid hepatic lesions. Normal hepatic parenchyma on CT. No suspicious hepatic lesions.
Spleen: No splenomegaly. No PSMA-avid splenic lesions.
Pancreas and adrenal glands: No PSMA-avid lesions.
Kidneys: Normal bilateral renal uptake (physiologic). No obstructive uropathy.
Aorta and retroperitoneum: No PSMA-avid retroperitoneal adenopathy.

PELVIS AND PELVIS/INGUINAL NODAL STATIONS:
Pelvic lymph nodes: Two discrete small PSMA-avid lymph nodes are identified in the right pelvis. The largest measures approximately 0.8 cm in short axis on CT and demonstrates clearly elevated PSMA activity (SUVmax approximately 6.7). A second adjacent node measures approximately 0.5 cm with SUVmax approximately 4.3. These nodes are in the right external iliac chain. The previously noted right external iliac adenopathy from prior imaging had decreased to 0.9 cm on ADT/abiraterone and was not clearly evaluable six months ago; the current PSMA-avid nodes represent either residual/recurrent or new nodal disease that was previously below the threshold of PSMA avidity or anatomic conspicuity.
Prostate bed: Irregular PSMA uptake in the prostate gland region consistent with known primary disease. No seminal vesicle invasion distinguishable at this point.

OSSEOUS DISEASE ON PSMA PET/CT:
All previously described bone lesions demonstrate PSMA avidity, confirming their metastatic nature:
- L3 vertebral body: Intense PSMA uptake, SUVmax 18.4 (markedly increased from prior scan 6 months ago where this lesion was sclerotic/treated in appearance)
- L4 vertebral body: PSMA-avid, SUVmax approximately 14.1
- Right iliac crest: PSMA-avid, SUVmax approximately 11.3
- Right sacral ala: PSMA-avid, SUVmax approximately 10.7
- Left acetabulum (new): PSMA-avid, SUVmax approximately 13.6
- Right femoral neck/intertrochanteric region (new): PSMA-avid, SUVmax approximately 15.2 — this new lesion in the proximal right femur is of particular note given the anatomic location in a load-bearing region
- T11 vertebral body (new): PSMA-avid, SUVmax approximately 9.2

VISCERAL ORGANS: No PSMA-avid hepatic, pulmonary, adrenal, or other visceral lesions identified on this examination.

--- IMPRESSION ---

1. PROGRESSIVE OSSEOUS METASTATIC DISEASE: Compared to prior bone scintigraphy approximately 6 months ago, there has been unequivocal progression of skeletal metastatic burden. Three new sites of osseous involvement are now identified: (a) right femoral neck and intertrochanteric region — a new, moderately intense lesion in a weight-bearing segment deserving close clinical attention; (b) T11 vertebral body — new lesion confirmed PSMA-avid; and (c) left acetabular/supra-acetabular region — new focus of uptake. Previously known lesions at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala appear larger and more metabolically active compared to prior, having lost their prior sclerotic/healing appearance. These findings are most consistent with active disease progression in the setting of enzalutamide resistance.

2. NEW PSMA-AVID PELVIC LYMPH NODES: Two small right external iliac chain lymph nodes demonstrate PSMA uptake above background, the largest approximately 0.8 cm with an SUVmax of 6.7. These represent new or newly apparent nodal metastases, consistent with nodal progression.

3. NO VISCERAL METASTASES DETECTED ON THIS EXAMINATION: There are no PSMA-avid hepatic, pulmonary, adrenal, or other visceral lesions identified. No peritoneal or retroperitoneal adenopathy.

4. ALL OSSEOUS AND NODAL LESIONS ARE STRONGLY PSMA-AVID, confirming active metastatic disease and suitability for PSMA-directed evaluation or therapy as clinically indicated.

5. The right proximal femoral lesion warrants orthopedic surgery consultation to assess structural integrity and fracture risk at this weight-bearing site.

Overall assessment: Widespread radiographically progressive metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with new osseous metastases and new pelvic nodal metastases, all strongly PSMA-avid. No visceral (liver, lung) disease on this study.
